content:
Traditionally, banks act as trusted middlemen, recording and verifying every transaction to ensure accuracy and prevent fraud. Blockchain changes this by removing the need for a central authority like a bank. Instead, it relies on a network of computers around the world that work together to confirm and record transactions. Each transaction is grouped into a “block” and added to a “chain” of previous transactions, creating a secure and transparent ledger visible to all participants.

Because this ledger is shared and constantly updated by many independent parties, it’s nearly impossible to alter past records without everyone noticing. This decentralized approach builds trust through transparency and collective verification, rather than relying on a single institution. For businesses, blockchain offers a reliable way to record transactions quickly and securely, reducing the need for intermediaries and increasing confidence in the data’s accuracy.
